elements.sent-2411-001: In the field of medical image segmentation, challenges such as indistinct lesion features, ambiguous boundaries, and multi-scale characteristics have long prevailed.
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper proposes an improved method named Intensity-Spatial Dual Masked AutoEncoder (ISD-MAE).
elements.sent-2411-003: Based on the tissue-contrast semi-masked autoencoder, a Masked AutoEncoder (MAE) branch is introduced to perform intensity masking and spatial masking operations on chest CT images for multi-scale feature learning and segmentation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-004: The model utilizes a dual-branch structure and contrastive learning to enhance the ability to learn tissue features and boundary details.
elements.sent-2411-005: Experiments are conducted on multiple 2D and 3D datasets.
elements.sent-2411-006: The results show that ISD-MAE significantly outperforms other methods in 2D pneumonia and mediastinal tumor segmentation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-007: For example, the Dice score reaches 90.10±0.54\% on the COVID19\_LESION dataset, and the performance is relatively stable.
elements.sent-2411-008: However, there is still room for improvement on 3D datasets.
elements.sent-2411-009: In response to this, improvement directions are proposed, including optimizing the loss function, using enhanced 3D convolution blocks, and processing datasets from multiple perspectives.Our code is available at: \url{https://github.com/prowontheus/ISD-MAE.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Medical images often exhibit complex structures and features.
elements.sent-2411-011: Self-supervised pretraining models can learn universal image features, such as texture, shape, and edges, from a large amount of unlabeled medical image data.
elements.sent-2411-012: These universal features are crucial for subsequent medical image segmentation tasks, providing the model with richer information.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, medical image processing faces various challenges, such as ambiguous lesion features, unclear boundaries, and multiscale features, which have long plagued researchers.
elements.sent-2411-014: To effectively overcome these challenges, researchers have been exploring various methods and techniques, with masking strategies gradually gaining attention as an important pretraining approach.
elements.figure-2411-001: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{Quick_overview.png}} \caption{An Overview of our ISD-MAE structure. \ First, the input image is fed into two branches. In the first branch, patches are randomly masked according to preset ratios and patch sizes to create a Spatial mask. In the other branch, grayscale values of the image are also randomly masked at preset ratios to create a intensity mask. Subsequently, these two corrupted images are input into the encoder to obtain feature embeddings for each branch. They are then separately fed into the decoder to reconstruct images for each branch. Finally, losses are calculated separately with the original image for each branch.} \label{fig1}
elements.sent-2411-015: Among the many self-supervised learning methods, Masked Image Modeling (MIM)\cite{b2}\cite{b3 is becoming increasingly popular.}
elements.sent-2411-016: By randomly masking parts of the image content and tasking the model to predict the masked portions based on the visible parts, the model can capture the correlations and dependencies between different regions in the image, thus gaining a more comprehensive understanding of the image's semantic information.
elements.sent-2411-017: In MIM, the model needs to learn both low-level features of the image (such as color, texture, etc.) and high-level semantic information (such as object categories, scene understanding, etc.).
elements.sent-2411-018: However, during training, the model may overly focus on reconstructing the masked pixel values and overlook learning the high-level semantic information.
elements.sent-2411-019: This could lead to poor performance of the model in downstream tasks, as these tasks rely more on the model's understanding of the high-level semantics of the image.
elements.sent-2411-020: Therefore, Huang et al. proposed Contrastive Masked AutoEncoder (CMAE)\cite{b1 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Building upon the foundation of masked image modeling, CMAE incorporates contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-022: It consists of two branches: an online branch for reconstructing masked images and a target branch that receives complete image inputs through momentum-updated encoders.
elements.sent-2411-023: Subsequently, the features from the online branch and the target branch are compared, enabling the model to learn not only how to reconstruct images but also the differences between different images, enhancing feature distinctiveness and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-024: In medical imaging, CT images are composed of the different Hounsfield Unit (HU) spectra, and HU values can help distinguish various human tissues.
elements.sent-2411-025: By observing HU values, it is possible to assist in determining the type of tissue present in the image, which is crucial for medical diagnosis, especially in detecting conditions such as tumors and cysts, where the HU values of the diseased tissue may differ from the surrounding normal tissue.
elements.sent-2411-026: To address this, Zheng et al. proposed the Tissue-Contrast Semi-Masked AutoEncoder (TCS-MAE)\cite{b4 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: This method utilizes a density-based masking strategy, randomly masking HU values at evenly spaced intervals based on the different characteristic ranges of HU spectra occupied by various tissues, and then reconstructing the masked tissue regions during training to enable the model to more effectively capture feature representations of different tissues.
elements.sent-2411-028: However, we have also found that spatial masking strategies have their unique advantages.
elements.sent-2411-029: Spatial masking divides the image into multiple small patches for masking, allowing the model to focus more on the local structure and detailed information of the image.
elements.sent-2411-030: For instance, in lung CT images, features such as small nodules and textures in the lungs are more easily learned and understood at the spatial level, while randomly masking HU values may potentially disrupt the integrity of this local structural information to some extent.
elements.sent-2411-031: Therefore, building upon the work of Zheng et al., we propose a novel pretraining method for chest CT images called Intensity-Spatial Dual Masked AutoEncoder (ISD-MAE).
elements.sent-2411-032: Based on TCS-MAE\cite{b4 , we introduce a second branch - the Masked AutoEncoder (MAE)\cite{b3 - and perform contrastive learning reconstruction of images through both branches to optimize the performance of medical image classification and segmentation tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: A simple overview is shown in Fig. \ref{fig1 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: We conducted experiments on three downstream tasks, including pneumonia detection, pneumonia segmentation, and mediastinal region segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-035: The experimental results indicate that our ISD-MAE method can more effectively learn tissue structure representations and significantly improve the classification and segmentation performance of downstream tasks compared to existing pretraining methods.
elements.sent-2411-036: The U-Net architecture, proposed by Ronneberger et al. \cite{b5 , is a classic structure in the field of medical image analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-037: This architecture consists of symmetric encoders and decoders.
elements.sent-2411-038: The encoder gradually reduces the spatial resolution of input images through a series of convolutional and pooling layers (downsampling layers), capturing high-level features such as edges, textures, and shapes.
elements.sent-2411-039: The decoder, on the other hand, restores the spatial resolution of the image through a series of transpose convolutional layers (upsampling layers) and convolutional layers.
elements.sent-2411-040: At each layer, the decoder concatenates feature maps from the corresponding encoder layer, allowing the transfer of low-level detail information to higher-level feature maps, ultimately reconstructing the feature maps to pixel-level predictions.
elements.sent-2411-041: In comparison to the U-Net, 3D U-Net utilizes three-dimensional convolution operations.
elements.sent-2411-042: Taking into account the global information of 3D data, it reduces the number of downsampling steps to avoid excessive loss of spatial information and decreases the network's parameter count.
elements.sent-2411-043: Zhou et al.\cite{b7 introduced a nested U-Net architecture called U-Net++.}
elements.sent-2411-044: This architecture nests the encoders and decoders of U-Net at multiple levels, with each level having its own encoder and decoder.
elements.sent-2411-045: Through cross-layer connections for information transfer, the network can better capture multi-scale information.
elements.sent-2411-046: Additionally, a dense connection mechanism is introduced, allowing each decoder layer to access information from all preceding encoder layers, enhancing feature propagation and utilization.
elements.sent-2411-047: The architecture of autoencoders originates from the fields of neural networks and unsupervised learning.
elements.sent-2411-048: Initially proposed by Hinton et al.\cite{b8 as stacked denoising autoencoders, the core idea is to reconstruct input data by learning compressed representations of the data, thereby achieving dimensionality reduction.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Subsequently, Vincent et al.\cite{b9 introduced a variant of autoencoders called "Denoising Autoencoder," aimed at learning robust feature representations of data.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Unlike traditional autoencoders, denoising autoencoders are trained by introducing noise (such as additive Gaussian noise) into the input data to learn feature representations robust to noise.
elements.sent-2411-051: Kingma et al.\cite{b10 combined autoencoders with probabilistic generative models and introduced a novel generative model known as the Variational Autoencoder (VAE), incorporating variational inference methods.}
elements.sent-2411-052: This approach trains the autoencoder by maximizing the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) to learn the latent distribution of data, enabling the generation of new data samples.
elements.sent-2411-053: This method provides an innovative approach for exploring the latent structure of data and generating new data samples.
elements.sent-2411-054: Contrastive Learning(CL) is a self-supervised learning method used for learning data representations, with the core idea of comparing the similarities and differences between different samples.
elements.sent-2411-055: Chen et al. \cite{b11 introduced the SimCLR framework for contrastive learning of visual representations.}
elements.sent-2411-056: This model aims to maximize the similarity of positive sample pairs and minimize the similarity of negative sample pairs to learn visual representations.
elements.sent-2411-057: SimCLR employs robust data augmentation strategies and contrastive loss functions, enabling the model to learn visually rich representations with semantic information from large-scale unlabeled image data.
elements.sent-2411-058: Moco\cite{b12 proposed by He et al. explores the path of learning visual representations through contrastive learning in the absence of label information.}
elements.sent-2411-059: This method leverages the idea of momentum contrast, introducing momentum updates during training to establish similarity relationships between positive and negative sample pairs.
elements.sent-2411-060: Grill et al. \cite{b13 explored the novel method of BYOL for contrastive self-supervised learning, training the model solely on different views of the same image (positive sample pairs) to learn similar representations of the same image from different perspectives, achieving self-supervised learning.}
elements.sent-2411-061: This method does not require learning from negative sample pairs, distinguishing it significantly from traditional contrastive learning methods \cite{b11}\cite{b2 .}
elements.sent-2411-062: Caron et al.\cite{b14 introduced the new features presented by self-supervised learning on the Vision Transformer (ViT) and proposed a concise and effective self-supervised learning method called self-distillation with no labels(DINO) .}
elements.sent-2411-063: This method combines techniques such as momentum encoders, multi-crop training, and small patches to learn useful image representations through knowledge distillation between student and teacher networks.
elements.sent-2411-064: Inspired by the aforementioned studies, some researchers have begun to apply contrastive learning and autoencoders to medical image research.
elements.sent-2411-065: For instance, Chaitanya et al.\cite{b15 introduced contrastive learning into the field of medical image segmentation for the first time, proposing a local contrastive loss to enhance the latent representations generated by the encoder, effectively alleviating the issue of insufficient annotated datasets in medical image analysis.Chen et al.\cite{b16 introduced Convolutional Autoencoder for Nodule Classification (CANN) for lung nodules.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: They successfully extracted accurate image features with only a small amount of data, enabling faster labeling of medical data.
elements.sent-2411-067: Additionally, CMAE\cite{b1 and TCS-MAE\cite{b4 also integrated contrastive learning and masked image modeling.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: However, these studies either focused solely on spatial-level masking methods or only considered the tissue distribution in medical images, without integrating both aspects comprehensively.
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{tabular}{c} \includegraphics[width=0.44\linewidth]{R_channel.png} (a) R-channel \end{tabular} \begin{tabular}{c} \includegraphics[width=0.44\linewidth]{G_channel.png} (b) G-channel \end{tabular} \begin{tabular}{c} \includegraphics[width=0.44\linewidth]{B_channel.png} (c) B-channel \end{tabular} \begin{tabular}{c} \includegraphics[width=0.44\linewidth]{Reconstructed_image.png} (d) RGB image \end{tabular} \caption{Displays the effects of the synthesis of the three channels and the RGB image using the method we have described. These three channels contain better contrast and richer edge information from the original image.} \label{fig2}
elements.sent-2411-069: Firstly, we resize the original image to change the image dimensions from (3, H, W) to (3, 256, 256).
elements.sent-2411-070: We then perform window width and level adjustment.
elements.sent-2411-071: The window width controls the grayscale range displayed, which affects the contrast of the display.
elements.sent-2411-072: A larger window width stretches the grayscale range displayed, making image details clearer and more visible.
elements.sent-2411-073: The window level sets the center grayscale value displayed, adjusting the window level can change the brightness of the image.
elements.sent-2411-074: Typically, 2D lung CT images have only one channel.
elements.sent-2411-075: Therefore, we combine the original lung image, mediastinal image, and edge image into three channels of an RGB image as input for the 2D image.
elements.sent-2411-076: The lung images and mediastinal images are derived from original 2D chest CT images through window width and level adjustments, while the edge image is obtained by sharpening with the Sobel operator, which can highlight the contours of tissue structures, aiding the model in better understanding the internal structures and boundaries of the image.
elements.sent-2411-077: The composition of the input RGB image is as follows: \begin{equation}\textbf{I} = (\textbf{I}_{lung}, \textbf{I}_{medi}, \textbf{I}_{edge})\label{eq1}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\textbf{I}_{lung} = (\frac{I_{ct} - L_{lung} + 0.5 * W_{lung}}{W_{lung}})*255\label{eq2}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\textbf{I}_{medi} = (\frac{I_{ct} - L_{medi} + 0.5 * W_{medi}}{W_{medi}})*255\label{eq3}\end{equation} Here, \( I_{ct \) represents the original image, where \( L_{lung = -500 \, \text{HU \), \( W_{lung = 1200 \, \text{HU \), \( L_{medi = 30 \, \text{HU \), and \( W_{medi = 300 \, \text{HU \).}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Setting these values can make the tissue structures, tumors, inflammations, and other lesion areas in the lungs clearer.
elements.sent-2411-079: The Sobel operator consists of a horizontal convolution kernel \( G_x \) and a vertical convolution kernel \( G_y \):.
elements.sent-2411-081: Finally, we combine the obtained edge images of the lung and mediastinum to obtain the final edge image: \begin{equation}\textbf{I}_{edge} = \max\{\textbf{I}^{lung}_{edge},\textbf{I}^{medi}_{edge}\} \label{eq7}\end{equation} By taking the maximum value of two images processed with the Sobel operator, not only can noise interference be reduced, but it also provides the network with more information and contrast.
elements.sent-2411-082: Fig. \ref{fig2 shows the effect of the synthesis of the three channels using the above method.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Similar to the preprocessing method for 2D images, 3D chest CT images also need to be adjusted from dimensions $(1, H, W, D)$ to $(1, 256, 256, 256)$.We adjust the thickness between layers in the H, W, and D directions using interpolation to a uniform thickness of 1mm.
elements.sent-2411-084: This helps reduce differences between images with varying layer thicknesses.
elements.sent-2411-085: Uniformly thick images typically exhibit a smoother and more continuous appearance, enhancing visual quality and leading to clearer and more accurate segmentation results.
elements.sent-2411-086: We then proceed to slice the 3D images layer by layer in the D dimension.
elements.sent-2411-087: Following this, we construct RGB channels for each image using the method described above before stacking the images layer by layer.
elements.sent-2411-088: Ultimately, our preprocessed images expand from a $(1, 256, 256, 256)$ dimension to $(3, 256, 256, 256)$.
elements.sent-2411-089: Assuming the input chest CT image is denoted as $I$, The method for generating an intensity mask on a Chest CT image can be described as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-090: Normalize the pixel values of the chest CT image $I$ to the range [0, 1]: \begin{equation}\textbf{I}_n = \frac{\textbf{I} - \min(\textbf{I})}{\max(\textbf{I}) - \min(\textbf{I})} \label{eq8}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-091: Convert the normalized image $I_n$ to a grayscale image $I_g$ using The luminosity method. \begin{equation}\textbf{I}_g = 0.2989 * \textbf{I}_n^r + 0.5870 * \textbf{I}_n^g + 0.1140 * \textbf{I}_n^b \label{eq9}\end{equation} Where, $I_n^r$, $I_n^g$, and $I_n^b$ represent the red, green, and blue channel slices of image $I_n$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-092: Divide the interval [0, 1] into $K$ equal partitions, which form the set of $S$.
elements.sent-2411-093: Here, $K$ is a hyperparameter predetermined regarding the mask scale.
elements.sent-2411-094: Subsequently, based on the intensity mask ratio parameter $r_t \in (0,1)$, randomly select $K \cdot r_t$ intervals from $S$ to compose the set $S_m$. \begin{equation}\textbf{S} = \{[\frac{i}{K},\frac{i+1}{K}] | i = 0,1,...,K-1\} \label{eq10}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\textbf{S}_m = \binom{|\textbf{S}|}{K \cdot r_t} \label{eq11}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-095: For each pixel $G_{i,j}$ in the grayscale image $I_g$, if it falls within any of the set $S_m$, set the RGB values at the corresponding position $(i,j)$ in the original image $I$ to zero: \begin{equation}\textbf{I}_{i,j} = \begin{cases} [0,0,0] \text{if } \textbf{G}_{i,j} \text{ falls in any of } \textbf{S}_m, \textbf{I}_{i,j} otherwise \end{cases} \label{eq12}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}\textbf{I}_{i,j} =  [0,0,0] if  \textbf{G}_{i,j}  falls in any of  \textbf{S}_m, \textbf{I}_{i,j} otherwise  \label{eq12}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-096: Ultimately, $I_{i,j}$ forms the image of the intensity mask image $I_m^t$.
elements.sent-2411-097: The method of spatial masking is consistent with the MAE\cite{b3 .}
elements.sent-2411-098: First, set hyperparameters $P$ and $r_t$, representing spatial mask size and mask ratio, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-099: Divide the image into grids of size $P$, then select a certain number of patches based on ratio $r_t$ and set the pixel values in these patches to 0.
elements.sent-2411-100: In the end, we obtain the image with spatial masking, denoted as $I_m^p$.
elements.sent-2411-102: Overall, the entire network follows the U-Net\cite{b5 architecture, but with slight differences.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Firstly, we replaced the encoder part with SegFormer, proposed by Xie et al\cite{b17 .}
elements.sent-2411-104: SegFormer is an efficient semantic segmentation framework that combines Transformers with lightweight multilayer perceptron (MLP) decoders.
elements.sent-2411-105: It does not require positional encoding and integrates local and global attention, enabling the efficient output of multiscale features.
elements.sent-2411-106: These characteristics make SegFormer superior to traditional U-Net encoders in feature extraction.
elements.sent-2411-107: After 5 rounds of downsampling, the size of the feature maps gradually decreases while the number of channels increases.
elements.sent-2411-108: The final layer's feature map has 512 channels, with an image size of $8*8$ (for 3D images, the size is $8*8*8$).
elements.sent-2411-109: Next, the feature map from the last layer is fed into the decoder, and nearest-neighbor interpolation is used for upsampling.
elements.sent-2411-110: The upsampled feature map is then concatenated with the corresponding feature map from the encoder's layer.
elements.sent-2411-111: After 5 rounds of such upsampling operations, the feature map size gradually recovers, and the number of channels decreases to 128, forming a decoder output of dimensions ($128, 256, 256$).
elements.sent-2411-112: Finally, after passing through a reconstruction head module, it is restored to the original image size of $(3, 256, 256)$.
elements.sent-2411-113: This process results in the reconstructed image.
elements.sent-2411-114: The first part is the similarity loss between the output of the decoder and the ground truth image, which utilizes the Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM) loss function\cite{b18 .}
elements.sent-2411-115: They are the output results of the intensity masked image and the spatial masked image passing through the model respectively.
elements.sent-2411-116: The second part of the loss function consists of the contrastive loss.
elements.sent-2411-117: The output feature map of the encoder goes through a project head module, which performs average pooling on the feature map, flattens it, and then passes it through a linear layer to obtain a 128-dimensional feature embedding.$F_t$ represents the embedding of the intensity mask image, and $F_p$ represents the embedding of the spatial mask image.
elements.sent-2411-118: Then we calculate the contrastive loss:.
elements.sent-2411-119: Normalize each vector $F_t$ and $F_p$ by their $\mathcal{L _2$ norms, and calculate the similarity score matrix for the two vectors: \begin{equation}\hat{\textbf{F}_t} = \frac{\textbf{F}_t}{\|\mathbf{\textbf{F}_t}\|_2}, \hat{\textbf{F}_p} = \frac{\textbf{F}_p}{\|\mathbf{\textbf{F}_p}\|_2} \label{eq15}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\hat{\textbf{S}} = logit\_scale \cdot (\hat{\textbf{F}_t} \cdot \hat{\textbf{F}_p^{\top}}) \label{eq16}\end{equation} $logit\_scale$ is a constant used to scale the similarity score matrix of embedding vectors, typically set to $\ln(\frac{1 {0.07 )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: Define the number of classes as $classes$, create class labels $labels = [0, 1, ..., classes-1]$, and calculate the cross-entropy loss twice.
elements.sent-2411-121: The final contrastive loss $\mathcal{L _{cons}$ is the average of the two cross-entropy losses. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_1 = \text{cross\_entropy}(\hat{\textbf{S}}, \text{labels}) \mathcal{L}_2 = \text{cross\_entropy}(\hat{\textbf{S}}^{\top}, \text{labels}) \end{aligned} \label{eq17}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{cons} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (\mathcal{L}_1 + \mathcal{L}_2) \label{eq18}\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation} L_1 = cross\_entropy(\hat{\textbf{S}}, labels) L_2 = cross\_entropy(\hat{\textbf{S}}^{\top}, labels)  \label{eq17}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-122: The loss function $\mathcal{L _{total}$ is the sum of the SSIM loss $\mathcal{L _{ssim}$ and the contrastive loss $\mathcal{L _{cons}$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{total} = \mathcal{L}_{ssim} + \mathcal{L}_{cons} \label{eq19}\end{equation}.}}}
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{The datasets used for validating the experiment.} \begin{center} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{3pt} { } { \begin{tabular}{@{}ccccccc@{}} Datasets Shape Samples Train/Test Annotation Regions Epochs Task TotalSegmentator\cite{b19} 2D/3D 1227/8805 - Without annotated regions 50 Pretraining COVID19\_LESION 2D 2729 4:1 pneumonia 30 Segmentation Task06\_Lung 2D 463 4:1 Lung cancer 100 Segmentation COVID19\_2D 2D 1655 4:1 pneumonia 40 Segmentation Lung\_nodule\_seg 2D 1657 4:1 Lung nodule 50 Segmentation Lung\_CT\_nodule 2D 920 4:1 Lung nodule 40 Segmentation LIDC\_IDRI 2D 1560 4:1 Lung nodule 40 Segmentation CT Scans for COVID19 2D 13980 4:1 pneumonia 20 Classification GRAM 2D 4163 4:1 Bacterial infections 50 Classification COVID19\_CT 3D 20 4:1 pneumonia 30 Segmentation COVID19\_3D 3D 1238 4:1 pneumonia 20 Segmentation \end{tabular} } \label{tab1} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-123: To validate our approach, we first conducted a pre-training task on the TotalSegmentator\cite{b19 lung CT scans, and then downstream segmentation and classification tasks were performed on eight 2D datasets and two 3D datasets, as shown in Table \ref{tab1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-124: Among these datasets, GRAM, COVID19\_3D, and COVID19\_2D are private datasets.
elements.sent-2411-125: These scans are derived from lung CT scans from multiple hospitals.
elements.sent-2411-126: The GRAM dataset consists of 2D images obtained by slicing the 3D lung CT dataset, comprising 4,163 slices with a thickness of 1mm.
elements.sent-2411-127: The COVID19\_3D dataset contains 1,238 images, while the COVID19\_2D dataset is derived from COVID19\_3D through slicing.
elements.sent-2411-128: We performed pretraining tasks on the chest 3D CT scans and their 2D slices of TotalSegmentator respectively.
elements.sent-2411-129: We ran 50 epochs on NVIDIA RTX 4070 ti super.
elements.sent-2411-130: The learning rate scheduler is StepLR.
elements.sent-2411-131: The step size is 100000 with one batch as one step.
elements.sent-2411-132: The decay rate is set to 0.96.
elements.sent-2411-134: The size of the intensity masking mask and the spatial masking mask are both 16.
elements.sent-2411-135: In downstream segmentation and classification tasks, we employed the StepLR learning rate scheduler with a step size of 1, invoking the step method once per training epoch, and a decay rate of 0.9.
elements.sent-2411-136: We utilized the AdamW optimizer and employed binary cross-entropy loss as the loss function.
elements.sent-2411-137: The ratio of training to testing sets across all datasets was set to approximately 4:1.
elements.sent-2411-138: Due to GPU memory constraints, the batch size was set to 16 for fine-tuning the 2D models and 1 for the 3D models.
elements.sent-2411-139: Additionally, we adopted mixed precision training, where model weights were stored in float32 during training, gradients were computed in float16, then converted back to single-precision floating point for parameter updates using the optimizer.
elements.sent-2411-140: We use Dice Similarity Coefficient(DSC) and Hausdorff distance (HD) to evaluate the performance of our pre-trained model on downstream segmentation tasks and ROC-AUC for evaluating classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-141: The Dice coefficient (DSC) is a metric used to assess the accuracy of image segmentation, commonly employed in the field of medical image segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-142: It measures the similarity of segmentation by calculating the ratio of the overlap between two segmented regions to their total volumes.
elements.sent-2411-143: The formula for the Dice coefficient is as follows: \begin{equation}\textbf{DSC}(\textbf{I}_{out}, \textbf{I}_g) = \frac{2 \times |\textbf{I}_{out} \cap \textbf{I}_g|}{|\textbf{I}_{out}| + |\textbf{I}_g|} \label{eq20}\end{equation} Where $I_{out}$ represents the region of the model's segmented result and $I_g$ represents the region of the ground truth segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-144: The Hausdorff distance is a measure describing the distance between two sets of points and is commonly used in fields such as image processing and computer vision to quantify the dissimilarity between two shapes or contours.The Hausdorff distance $H(I_{out ,I_g)$ is defined as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \textbf{H}(\textbf{I}_{out},\textbf{I}_g) = \max(h(\textbf{I}_{out},\textbf{I}_g),h(\textbf{I}_g,\textbf{I}_{out})) h(\textbf{I}_{out},\textbf{I}_g) = \max_{a\in\textbf{I}_{out}}\min_{b\in \textbf{I}_g}\|a - b\|) h(\textbf{I}_g,\textbf{I}_{out}) = \max_{b\in \textbf{I}_g}\min_{a\in \textbf{I}_{out}}\|b - a\|) \end{aligned} \label{eq21}\end{equation} where $(\|\cdot\|)$ represents a distance metric between two points, typically the Euclidean distance.}
elements.sent-2411-145: In Table \ref{tab2 , we present a comparison of the downstream segmentation results on a series of chest CT datasets using our ISD-MAE and numerous state-of-the-art self-supervised learning models.}
elements.sent-2411-146: The data in the table represents the segmentation Dice scores of different models on various datasets.
elements.sent-2411-147: The models compared include AE\cite{b20 , DAE\cite{b21 , MAE\cite{b3 , CMAE\cite{b1 , CAE\cite{b22 , and MaskFeat\cite{b23 .}}}}}}
elements.subsection-2411-011: Comparison to state of the art methods
elements.sent-2411-148: From the segmentation results in Table \ref{tab2 , it can be seen that ISD-MAE's Dice scores are generally higher than those of other methods across all pneumonia segmentation datasets (COVID1\_LESION, COVID19\_2D, COVID19\_CT, COVID19\_3D), especially in the COVID19\_LESION dataset, where its Dice score reaches as high as 90.10±0.54\%, significantly exceeding the scores of other methods, and the Hausdorff distance values are comparatively lower as well.}
elements.sent-2411-149: In the mediastinal tumor datasets (Task06\_Lung, Lung\_nodule\_seg, Lung\_CT\_nodule, LIDC\_IDRI), ISD-MAE also performs well, particularly in the Lung\_nodule\_seg and Lung\_CT\_nodule datasets, with Dice scores of 93.37±1.55\% and 92.11±2.23\% respectively, also higher than other methods.
elements.sent-2411-150: Looking at the standard deviation, ISD-MAE exhibits relatively small standard deviations across multiple datasets, indicating its performance is relatively stable, less affected by data fluctuations, which is crucial for tasks requiring high-precision predictions like medical image segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-151: The performance of ISD-MAE on 3D images, such as COVID19\_CT, while not as high as in 2D images, is still competitive.
elements.sent-2411-152: This also demonstrates the potential and advantages of ISD-MAE in handling complex, multi-dimensional medical imaging data, as well as its strong adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-153: The mediastinal region contains multiple important organs and tissues, often closely adjacent with indistinct boundaries, leading to irregular shapes and blurred edges.
elements.sent-2411-154: ISD-MAE specifically masks intensity and spatial aspects, focusing on local tumor details such as texture and edges at the spatial level.
elements.sent-2411-155: Capturing these local features is particularly crucial for irregularly shaped mediastinal tumors.
elements.sent-2411-156: At the tissue level, considering the overall relationship between the tumor and surrounding tissues and obtaining contextual information can help determine the tumor's position and boundaries more accurately.
elements.sent-2411-157: Pneumonia manifestations in chest CT images are relatively straightforward and may not require such a detailed multi-layered masking strategy for effective segmentation, causing ISD-MAE's advantages to not be fully realized in such scenarios.
elements.figure-2411-006: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{Segmentation_results.png}} \caption{Segmentation Result Visualization. \ We compared the visualization results on pneumonia and mediastinal tumor datasets for CMAE, DAE, CAE, AE, and our ISD-MAE. It is evident that our ISD-MAE has a clear advantage in delineating the boundary of lesion areas. } \label{fig4}
elements.sent-2411-158: This highlights ISD-MAE's robustness and effectiveness in handling complex medical imaging data and its ability to generalize well across different datasets.
elements.sent-2411-159: Moreover, the high ROC-AUC scores indicate the model's strong predictive capability and its potential for accurate classification tasks in medical image analysis.
elements.sent-2411-160: It can be observed that compared to the other 4 methods (CMAE, DAE, CAE, AE), ISD-MAE may more accurately capture the boundaries and details of the lesion area.
elements.sent-2411-161: Particularly, it performs well in segmenting diverse contour areas such as pneumonia (COVID19\_2D) and can identify tumors of various shapes and sizes in the Lung\_CT\_nodule dataset.
elements.sent-2411-162: This suggests that our ISD-MAE has learned more general image features, indicating better generalization ability.
elements.sent-2411-163: The Fig \ref{fig5 depicts the online evaluation results of DSC on ISD-MAE, CMAE, and AE for four segmentation tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-164: These tasks include pneumonia (COVID19\_LESION, COVID19\_CT) and mediastinal tumors (Lung\_nodule\_seg and Lung\_CT\_nodule), where COVID19\_CT is a 3D dataset.
elements.sent-2411-165: From the graph, it can be observed that for the tasks of COVID19\_LESION and Lung\_nodule\_seg, the Dice coefficient of ISD-MAE consistently surpasses CMAE and AE, reaching over 80\% DSC almost in the initial stages.
elements.sent-2411-166: For the Lung\_CT\_nodule dataset, ISD-MAE initially scores lower in the first two epochs but quickly surpasses CMAE and AE thereafter.
elements.sent-2411-167: ISD-MAE may require more time in the initial stages to adapt to the dataset's characteristics, hence showing suboptimal performance in the first few epochs.
elements.sent-2411-168: However, once the model begins to converge, its performance rapidly improves and surpasses other methods.
elements.figure-2411-007: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{DSC_performance.png}} \caption{The online evaluate of DSC scores for pneumonia and mediastinal tumors. It can be seen that our ISD-MAE demonstrates significant advantages across multiple tasks, especially in the early stages, quickly achieving optimal results with only a few epochs.} \label{fig5}
elements.figure-2411-008: \caption{The impact of different masking strategies on downstream segmentation tasks. "Intensity mask only" represents using only intensity masking strategy, while "Spatial mask only" represents using only spatial masking strategy. In each cell of the table, the number above represents the Dice similarity coefficient (\%), while the number below represents the Hausdorff distance (HD). The best score in each column is shown in bold.} \begin{center} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.5} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{3pt} { } { \begin{tabular}{@{}c|cccccccc@{}} \diagbox{Strategy}{Datasets} COVID19\_LESION Task06\_Lung COVID19\_2D Lung\_nodule\_seg Lung\_CT\_nodule LIDC\_IDRI COVID19\_CT COVID19\_3D \multirow{2}{*}{Intensity mask only} $85.02\pm0.67$ $\mathbf{87.42\pm0.89}$ $\mathbf{79.89\pm1.96}$ $89.45\pm0.66$ $84.35\pm0.46$ $86.55\pm0.84$ $\mathbf{83.68\pm2.84}$ $\mathbf{67.47\pm0.60}$ $22.71\pm0.19$ $7.87\pm0.11$ $23.09\pm0.18$ $4.63\pm0.13$ $14.12\pm0.37$ $8.02\pm0.21$ $14.79\pm0.77$ $\mathbf{21.26\pm0.72}$ \multirow{2}{*}{Spatial mask only} $\mathbf{85.65\pm0.45}$ $85.78\pm0.54$ $79.12\pm1.84$ $\mathbf{90.33\pm0.78}$ $\mathbf{85.33\pm0.64}$ $\mathbf{87.05\pm0.75}$ $82.2\pm3.42$ $67.08\pm0.64$ $\mathbf{21.78\pm0.22}$ $\mathbf{7.41\pm0.09}$ $\mathbf{23.01\pm0.19}$ $\mathbf{4.24\pm0.11}$ $\mathbf{13.85\pm0.28}$ $\mathbf{8.00\pm0.17}$ $\mathbf{14.62\pm0.64}$ $21.39\pm0.68$ \end{tabular} } \label{tab3} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-169: However, ISD-MAE does not exhibit significant advantages over the other two methods on the 3D dataset (COVID19\_CT).
elements.sent-2411-170: Despite ISD-MAE's excellent performance on 2D datasets, this does not imply seamless scalability to higher-dimensional datasets.
elements.sent-2411-171: The complexity of 3D datasets is much higher than that of 2D datasets, with 3D images containing more spatial and structural information.
elements.sent-2411-172: The shape and location of tumor or lesion areas may vary more in three-dimensional space, and there is contextual information between adjacent slices that the model needs to effectively capture and utilize.
elements.sent-2411-173: Additionally, 3D image data is massive, requiring substantial computational resources and memory for processing and segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-174: Directly handling the entire 3D image may exceed hardware limitations, necessitating strategies such as segmenting the 3D image into sub-volumes for processing, which may lead to some loss of spatial information.
elements.sent-2411-175: The design of ISD-MAE may encounter scalability bottlenecks when dealing with such 3D data, resulting in its performance not significantly surpassing other methods.
elements.figure-2411-009: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{Loss_curve.png}} \caption{The loss function curves for pre-trained images on the TotalSegmentator dataset using three different methods (ISD-MAE, CMAE, and AE).} \label{fig6}
elements.sent-2411-176: During the pre-training of images, we plotted the loss function curves(Fig. \ref{fig6 ) for the ISD-MAE, CMAE, and AE methods to evaluate their performance during the training process.}
elements.sent-2411-177: In the initial stages, the loss function value of ISD-MAE rapidly decreases from a higher level with a significant magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-178: In comparison, the initial loss function value of CMAE is slightly lower than ISD-MAE, with a relatively slower decrease rate; while AE starts with a higher initial loss function value and a slower decrease rate.
elements.sent-2411-179: As training progresses, the loss function of ISD-MAE continues to steadily decrease, reaching a lower level (approximately 0.1) around 300 steps.
elements.sent-2411-180: The loss function of CMAE also steadily decreases, but its value at 300 steps is still higher than ISD-MAE (approximately 0.18).
elements.sent-2411-181: AE shows an accelerated decrease in this phase, but the overall loss function value remains higher than ISD-MAE and CMAE.
elements.sent-2411-182: In the later stages of training, the loss functions of the three methods tend to stabilize, with CMAE's final stabilized value slightly higher than ISD-MAE and also higher than AE.
elements.sent-2411-183: To evaluate the contributions of intensity masks and spatial masks to model performance, we designed a series of experiments.
elements.sent-2411-184: For instance, while keeping other components constant, we trained the model using only intensity masks or spatial masks and observed changes in metrics such as Dice coefficient and HD in downstream segmentation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-185: From the table, it can be observed that for 2D tasks in pneumonia segmentation, the intensity masking strategy performs better in terms of the Dice coefficient, but the spatial masking strategy excels in the Hausdorff distance (HD), indicating more accurate boundary segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-186: For 3D tasks (COVID19 CT, COVID19 3D), although the difference in Dice coefficients is not significant, the spatial masking strategy still performs better in HD.
elements.sent-2411-187: For the lung nodule dataset, the advantage of the spatial masking strategy is more pronounced, with three lung nodule datasets showing higher Dice coefficients and Hausdorff distances under the spatial masking strategy.
elements.sent-2411-188: The spatial masking strategy excels in the Hausdorff distance (HD), indicating more accurate boundary segmentation results.
elements.sent-2411-189: Therefore, considering a combination of both masking strategies can leverage their respective strengths, making the model more robust and adaptable to different datasets and tasks.
elements.sent-2411-190: Although ISD-MAE excels on traditional 2D images, it lacks significant advantages on 3D datasets.
elements.sent-2411-191: The multi-level masking strategy of ISD-MAE may not be flexible or effective enough in 3D space to fully capture the complex features within 3D images.
elements.sent-2411-192: For improvements in this aspect, we believe that work can be expanded in three main areas:.
elements.sent-2411-193: Consider utilizing multi-scale SSIM loss functions and combining them with weighted aggregation for a more comprehensive assessment of structural similarity.
elements.sent-2411-194: Alternatively, integrate DICE loss to simultaneously consider both structural similarity and region overlap in images.
elements.sent-2411-196: Explore the use of deformable convolutions\cite{b24 , depthwise separable convolutions\cite{b25 , etc., which offer better robustness in handling images with scale and rotation changes compared to traditional convolutions, with increased computational efficiency.}}
elements.sent-2411-197: Datasets: Process 3D images from multiple perspectives (such as axial, coronal, and sagittal views) and enhance segmentation performance by fusing information from these views.
elements.sent-2411-198: This can be achieved through simple feature concatenation, weighted averaging, or attention mechanisms for integration.
elements.sent-2411-199: This paper proposes an improved self-supervised learning method, namely Intensity-Spatial Dual Masked AutoEncoder (ISD-MAE), which introduces a Masked Autoencoder (MAE) branch in the model to perform intensity masking and spatial masking operations on images for multi-scale feature learning and segmentation tasks in chest CT images.
elements.sent-2411-200: By incorporating a dual-branch structure and contrastive learning, the model's ability to learn tissue features and boundary details is enhanced.
elements.sent-2411-201: Experimental results demonstrate that ISD-MAE outperforms other methods significantly in segmentation tasks on 2D pneumonia and mediastinal tumor datasets, but there is still room for improvement in performance on 3D datasets.
elements.sent-2411-202: This work is supported by Computer Architecture Laboratory of Zhejiang University and the Supercomputing Center of Hangzhou City University.
elements.sent-2411-204: Firstly, we replaced the encoder part with SegFormer, proposed by Xie et al.\cite{b17 SegFormer is an efficient semantic segmentation framework that combines Transformers with lightweight multilayer perceptron (MLP) decoders.}
